One local auto dealership experienced damage to 297 cars on the lot.  Hundreds of windows were lost on the campus of the University of Nebraska-Kearney.  Eight police cruisers were damaged.  Fifteen (15) people were treated for minor abrasions and released from the local hospital.  Several hailstones measured 5 inches in diameter throughout the county, and there were unconfirmed reports of melon sized hail near Amherst.In Kearney county, extensive damage was noted in the Minden area.  Four and a half (4.5) inch diameter hail was reported in the city.  The most extensive damage occurred at the indoor greenhouse facility which grows tomatoes.  The glass structure was no match for the softball size hailstones.  2500 pains of glass were broken, including about 60 percent of the roof panes.  Shards of glass penetrated the tomatoes and forced the expulsion of the product for safety reasons.  Crop damage was widespread throughout the county as over 22,000 acres of corn, soybeans and alfalfa were severely damaged or destroyed.  The storm continued south and produced more crop and property damage in parts of Webster and Franklin counties.

During the early afternoon hours on Monday, August 26th, thunderstorms developed rapidlyalong a thunderstorm outflow boundary in Dundy and Hitchcock counties in SW Nebraska.  Thestorms quickly intensified to severe levels, with the first report of large hail occurring at 140 pmMDT in SE Dundy County. During the next 30 minutes, reports of softball-size hail (4"diameter), 87 mph winds and a tornado were received.  For the next six hours, a small cluster ofthunderstorms approximately two counties wide continued to produce violent weather acrosswestern Kansas as the storms moved south at speeds ranging from 5 mph on the eastern extent ofthe cluster, to 50 mph on the western extent.

An ominous looking supercell dived southeast from around Ord to southwest of Hastings spawning tornadoes and damaging hail along its path.  The structure of the severe thunderstorm was easily visible from many miles away.  After producing large hail in northwest Valley county, a tornado was spotted by a volunteer fireman north of Arcadia.  No damaged occurred.  As the storm drove southeast, hail to the size of baseballs was reported from near Ravenna to south of Shelton.  To the northeast of Shelton, another tornado was reported to have lasted about four minutes.  No property damage was noted.  Wind driven large hail did hammer much of Shelton and the surrounding areas.  Windows were blown out, cars severely damaged and many roofs were deemed lost.  Some crops in the area were leveled as damage totaled well into the millions.  The storm continued to pound western Adams and eastern Kearney county with damaging hail and wind before falling apart in Franklin county around midnight.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local radio station reported 60 to 70 mph wind gusts in and near Gering, NE.
Hail from nickel to golfball size reported in Sidney, NE.

Four and a half (4.5) inch diameter hail was reported in the city.  The most extensive damage occurred at the indoor greenhouse facility which grows tomatoes.  The glass structure was no match for the softball size hailstones.  2500 pains of glass were broken, including about 60 percent of the roof panes.  Shards of glass penetrated the tomatoes and forced the expulsion of the product for safety reasons.  Crop damage was widespread throughout the county as over 22,000 acres of corn, soybeans and alfalfa were severely damaged or destroyed.  The storm continued south and produced more crop and property damage in parts of Webster and Franklin counties.

Thunderstorm winds caused widespread damage to trees and structures. The worst structural damage was on a farm just south of Jackson where two barns and a windmill were totally destroyed, shingles on the roof of the house were blown off, an air conditioner was destroyed, five horses were injured, and electricity was cut off. In Jackson, shingles were blown off the roofs of buildings, siding was damaged from wind blown debris, and a few homes suffered damage when large branches fell. Major tree damage in Jackson included a few large trees blown over. Power lines and poles were blown down or damaged by falling trees, resulting in power outages.
Large hail, driven by severe winds, damaged siding on homes, and also dented vehicles.

The River Road south of Oshkosh washed out in numerous locations due to fast flowing water rushing over the road.
Lightning struck the TV antenna on a house and then traveled through the wiring in the house.  The house received extensive fire, heat and smoke damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter estimated thunderstorm winds of at least 80 mph.  The winds snapped 7 large multi-tiered and multi-line power poles.  An 18 inch diameter tree was snapped 2 feet above the ground.  Numerous power lines and smaller trees were downed within a half mile of the destroyed power poles.  A metal roof was also torn off the north side of a barn.
Hail up to 1.25 inches in diameter was reported in Columbus by emergency management.

A thunderstorm wind gust of 61 mph was recorded by the AWOS at the Columbus airport.  This is from the same storm that produced hail up to 1.25 inches in diameter.  The winds downed large tree limbs in town, several of which fell on vehicles or downed a few power lines.
A storm spotter estimated thunderstorm wind gusts of around 60 mph.
A thunderstorm downburst downed several large trees on the north side of Beemer.
Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage. The amount of damage was not known.
Large hail accumulated to a depth of three inches.
Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including trees and large limbs blown down. The winds also broke a window in a police building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms developed during the evening hours across portions of south-central Nebraska.  The storms started out just south of Grand Island and spread east.  Two tornadoes were observed from these storms.  The first was in northern Clay county and was reported by a storm chaser to have set down for about a mile.  The second was observed by an off duty NWS meteorologist and was only one the ground for a brief time.  No damage occurred from either tornado.  Very heavy rains were reported across Hamilton county were some flooding occurred.  Numerous hail reports from the area indicated that stones ranged from the size of nickels to golf balls.

Thunderstorm winds caused damage to 10,000 acres of crops. A few fields sustained extensive damage, but most was leaf damage with hope of some recovery. Most of the damage was to corn crops, while soybean crops withstood the strong winds with minor damage. No damage estimate could be given because it was not known how much the crops would recover.

Thunderstorm winds caused widespread tree damage, including some large trees blown down. Some of the tree debris caused damage to homes. The winds also caused other damage to buildings, including a garage which was destroyed, a shed overturned, windows broken, and roof and siding damage. Power lines and poles were blown down, resulting in power outages. A woman suffered a back injury and a broken arm when hit by debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain producing thunderstorms tracked across parts of southeast and east central Nebraska as well as southwest Iowa late Thursday evening (8/22) into Friday morning (8/23).  For some locations this was the second night in a row for heavy rain.  Heavy rain amounts for the 24 hours ending around 7am cdt on the 23rd included...4.3 inches at Crete, 4.18 inches in Plattsmouth, 3.5 inches in Springfield, 3.23 inches at Wahoo, 3.14 inches at Glenwood, Iowa, and between 2.5 and 2.75 inches at Yutan, Ithaca and Gretna along with Hamburg, Iowa.Flooding reports included...In Cass county, Many streets were flooded in Plattsmouth and 1 citizen had to be rescued from a car stalled in the water.  Mill creek near Louisville flooded highway 50 near town sending logs onto the highway.  Highway 66 between Louisville and the Cedar Creek Spur also flooded.  Scenic Drive, 1 mile east of Weeping Water, also flooded.In Sarpy county, flood waters sent water and mud 2 to 3 feet deep across highway 6 southwest of Gretna.In Saunders county, the flooding washed out part of a railroad bridge near the intersection of highways 6 and 63 near Ashland.In Lancaster county, flood waters did serious erosion to several county roads from 3 miles west of Emerald to 7 miles southwest of Sprague.Otoe county reported that Owl Creek flooded county roads northeast of Palmyra.In Saline county, flood waters flowed at least 1 foot deep across the intersection of highways 33 and 103 near Crete causing a temporary closing of the highways.  At least 10 streets were closed for a while in Crete and water entered basements of a few homes and an elementary school.

Hail up to golfball size was accompanied by thunderstorm wind gusts around 60 mph.  There were unofficial reports that some of the hail was up to baseball size.  The hail did extensive damage to 224 homes in Waverly, damaging roofs, knocking holes in windows and causing siding damage.  The hail also damaged many cars.  The strong winds toppled 2 trees in town, and combined with the hail to strip a significant number of leaves off of many trees.  Three firefighters were slightly injured by the hail, two by direct hits and one after he slipped on the hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm downburst with winds estimated at over 70 mph caused a 2 to 3 mile wide damage path from just north of Elkhorn to near Bennington.  Much of the wind damage was fallen trees, however some houses were damaged by fallen trees or sustained severe siding, roof and window damage due to wind driven hail.  The first report of significant damage was 2 miles north of Elkhorn where trees were blown down in 5 adjoining yards.  As the storm progressed to near Bennington, a semi trailer truck was overturned on highway 36, while trees were blown across 108th and highway 36 and at 186th and Bennington Road.  Eight power poles were also downed near 192nd and Military.  The winds also destroyed a house under construction near Bennington, and caused roof damages to houses south of Bennington.

Thunderstorm downburst winds produced a 5 to 6 mile wide path of damage from around 5 miles southwest of Ft. Calhoun into the Ft. Calhoun area.  The wind damage was from the same storm complex that produced the damage in northwest Douglas county.  Winds with this damage path were estimated by the NWS to be stronger than those observed in Douglas county, perhaps around 100 mph.  The winds caused extensive tree damage and also destroyed a house just northwest of Ft. Calhoun causing 2 minor injuries.  Windows were reported to be blown out by the winds and wind driven hail also caused substantial window, roof and siding damage with an estimated 85 percent of all homes in town damaged to some degree.  One woman required emergency room treatment after being caught out in the open and pelted with hail up to quarter size.  The storm caused an estimated 3 million dollars worth of damage.
Reported near 27th and highway 2.

Thunderstorm winds blew 2 large grain bins across 176th Street at least 100 yards from their foundations. One of them was an 8000 bushel model which was blown one-fourth of a mile causing tree and house damage to the farmstead it hit.  Other farms around the area sustained tree and outbuilding damage due to the strong winds including three antique railroad cars that were blown over at a local show ground.  The winds also downed 6 miles of power lines and broke large tree limbs which damaged a car and 2 houses near Waverly.
Reported near 132nd and Maple.
Hail up to 1 inch in diameter was reported from Wilber through the De Witt areas.
Thunderstorm winds blew down 3 trees in the Hickman area.
Thunderstorm wind gusts around 60 mph broke large tree limbs near and west of Plattsmouth.
Thunderstorm winds downed 6 to 8 inch diameter tree limbs in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper level low pressure system moved out of Colorado and across western Nebraska.  Rain changed to snow quickly across the eastern portion of the Nebraska Panhandle and into portions of western and north central Nebraska allowing significant snowfall accumulations.  One location in southern Cherry County reported 12 inches of snow.  Over the rest of the area, wind gusts to 35 mph combined with 5 to 7 inches of snow to create low visibilities with blowing and drifting snow.  Four to five foot snow drifts blocked U.S. Highway 30 in Deuel County of the southeast Nebraska Panhandle.
